summary: O'Neil identifies four distinct political camps in the big data world: corporate data collectors, security advocates, open data proponents, and civil rights advocates concerned with how data affects marginalized populations. She attended a UC Berkeley conference that unusually brought these camps together for cross-boundary dialogue. Each camp has different definitions of risk and different priorities, leading to divergent regulatory proposals. O'Neil positions herself in the civil rights camp, focusing on how big data disproportionately harms poor people through targeted scams and predatory practices. While the conference reached no firm conclusions, she values the conversation and calls for continued public debate between these groups.
key_points: The big data world contains four camps with distinct interests: corporate profit-maximizers, security/surveillance critics, open data advocates, and civil rights defenders
Each camp defines risks differently, leading to fundamentally different policy prescriptions and priorities
The civil rights camp focuses on how data systems harm vulnerable populations in daily life, not just dramatic surveillance scenarios
Open data advocates are split between activists seeking transparency and government officials promoting win-win narratives
Corporate interests were notably absent from the Berkeley conference, busy lobbying against regulation in Washington
money_quotes: I care about hackers and people unreasonably tracked and targeted by the NSA, but I don't think about that nearly as much as I think about how easy it is for poor people to be targeted by scam operations when they google for 'how do I get food stamps'.
The corporate big data camp was not represented in that room as far as I know, but that's because they're too busy lobbying for a continuation of little to no regulation in Washington.
There is no one ideal regulation that will fix everything, and for that matter some people involved don't believe that government regulations can ever work.

All of these camps have their various interests, and can make good arguments for them. Even more importantly, they each have their own definition of the risks, as well as the probability of those risks.

The people who worry about how this avalanche of big data is affecting the daily lives of citizens, not only when we are targeted by the NSA or by someone stealing our credit cards, but when we are born poor versus rich.
key_insight: Different stakeholders in the big data debate aren't just disagreeing about solutions—they have fundamentally different definitions of what the risks are and who is most harmed.
